content:
# Mark Chapter 10 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 37v–38r (PDF pages 74–75) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_010_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_010_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ Jesus left there and went into the region of Judea and across the Jordan. Once again crowds gathered around him, and as usual, he taught them.

² Some Pharisees came to test him with a question: "Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife?"

³ "What did Moses command you?" he asked.

⁴ They said, "Moses allowed a man to write a divorce certificate and send her away."

⁵ Jesus said, "Moses wrote that rule because your hearts are hard.

⁶ But from the very beginning of creation, God 'made them male and female.'

⁷ 'That is why a man leaves his father and mother and is united with his wife,

⁸ and the two become one.' They are no longer two separate people but one.

⁹ So what God has joined together, no one should tear apart."

¹⁰ Back in the house, the disciples brought it up again.

¹¹ He told them, "Anyone who divorces his wife and marries another woman commits adultery against her.

¹² And if a wife divorces her husband and marries another man, she commits adultery."

¹³ People started bringing their children to Jesus for him to touch and bless them, but the disciples shooed them away.

¹⁴ When Jesus saw this, he was angry. "Let the children come to me!" he said. "Don't stop them! The kingdom of God belongs to people like these.

¹⁵ I tell you the truth: anyone who doesn't receive God's kingdom the way a child does will never enter it."

¹⁶ Then he picked up the children one by one, held them in his arms, and blessed them, placing his hands on each one.

¹⁷ As Jesus was heading out, a man ran up and dropped to his knees in front of him. "Good Teacher," he asked, "what must I do to inherit eternal life?"

¹⁸ "Why do you call me good?" Jesus replied. "No one is good except God alone.

¹⁹ You know the commandments: Don't murder. Don't commit adultery. Don't steal. Don't lie under oath. Don't cheat people. Honor your father and mother."

²⁰ "Teacher," the man said, "I've obeyed all of these since I was a boy."

²¹ Jesus looked straight at him—and loved him. Then he said, "There's one thing you're missing. Go sell everything you own, give the money to the poor, and you'll have treasure in heaven. Then come and follow me."

²² The man's face fell. He walked away devastated, because he was extremely wealthy.

²³ Jesus looked around at his disciples and said, "How hard it is for the rich to enter God's kingdom!"

²⁴ The disciples were stunned. But Jesus repeated it: "Children, how hard it is to enter the kingdom of God!

²⁵ It's easier to thread a camel through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to get into God's kingdom."

²⁶ They were completely floored. "Then who in the world can be saved?" they asked each other.

²⁷ Jesus looked at them and said, "Humanly speaking, it's impossible. But not with God. Everything is possible with God."

²⁸ Peter spoke up: "We've given up everything to follow you."

²⁹ Jesus said, "I promise you: everyone who has given up home, brothers, sisters, mother, father, children, or property for my sake and for the good news

³⁰ will receive back a hundred times as much in this life—homes, brothers, sisters, mothers, children, and property—along with persecution. And in the coming age, eternal life.

³¹ But many who seem to be first will end up last, and the last will be first."

³² They were on the road heading up to Jerusalem, with Jesus walking ahead of them. The disciples were stunned, and those following behind were afraid. He pulled the twelve aside once more and began telling them what was about to happen:

³³ "Listen—we're going up to Jerusalem. The Son of Man will be handed over to the chief priests and the scholars. They'll condemn him to death and turn him over to the Romans.

³⁴ They'll ridicule him, spit on him, flog him, and kill him. But three days later, he will rise."

³⁵ James and John, Zebedee's sons, approached him. "Teacher," they said, "we want you to do something for us."

³⁶ "What is it?" he asked.

³⁷ "When you come into your glory, let one of us sit at your right and the other at your left."

³⁸ Jesus said, "You have no idea what you're asking. Can you drink the cup I'm going to drink? Can you be plunged into the suffering I'm about to endure?"

³⁹ "We can," they said. Jesus told them, "You will indeed drink my cup and share my suffering.

⁴⁰ But seats at my right and left aren't mine to hand out. Those places are for the ones they've been prepared for."

⁴¹ When the other ten heard about this, they were furious with James and John.

⁴² Jesus gathered them all together and said, "You know how it works in the world—rulers throw their weight around, and powerful people push everyone else down.

⁴³ That's not how it will be among you. Whoever wants to be great must become a servant.

⁴⁴ Whoever wants to be first must become everyone's slave.

⁴⁵ The Son of Man didn't come to be served—he came to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many."

⁴⁶ They arrived at Jericho. As Jesus was leaving the city with his disciples and a large crowd, a blind beggar named Bartimaeus—son of Timaeus—was sitting by the road.

⁴⁷ When he heard it was Jesus of Nazareth, he started yelling, "Jesus! Son of David! Have mercy on me!"

⁴⁸ People told him to shut up. But he shouted even louder, "Son of David, have mercy on me!"

⁴⁹ Jesus stopped. "Bring him here," he said. They called to the blind man: "Cheer up! On your feet—he's calling for you!"

⁵⁰ Bartimaeus threw off his cloak, jumped up, and made his way to Jesus.

⁵¹ "What do you want me to do for you?" Jesus asked. The blind man said, "Master, I want to see."

⁵² "Go," Jesus said. "Your faith has healed you." Instantly the man could see, and he followed Jesus down the road.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **"Jesus looked at him—and loved him" (v. 21):** Only Mark records this. One of the most tender moments in the Gospels. Jesus isn't angry at the rich man. He loves him—and then asks him to do the hardest thing in the world. Love doesn't always make things easier.

2. **"Along with persecution" (v. 30):** Notice the realism. The hundredfold blessing comes "with persecutions." Jesus never promises his followers an easy ride. Mark's audience, facing Roman hostility, would have heard this with sharp recognition.

3. **The cup and the baptism (v. 38):** "Cup" is Old Testament language for God's wrath (Isaiah 51:17; Jeremiah 25:15). "Baptism" here means being overwhelmed by suffering. James and John cheerfully promise they can handle it—James became the first apostle martyred (Acts 12:2).

4. **"Ransom for many" (v. 45):** This is Mark's theological summary of Jesus's mission. The word λύτρον comes from the slave trade—the price paid to free a captive. "For many" (ἀντὶ πολλῶν) implies substitution. Jesus dies in the place of others.

5. **Bartimaeus throws off his cloak (v. 50):** A beggar's cloak was his most valuable possession—his shelter, his bed, his identity. Throwing it off is an act of total faith. He believes he won't need it anymore.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
